1: 'What causes quarrels and conflicts among you? Is it not your passions that are at war within you?' — the internal war of hēdonai (pleasures, lusts) that produces external conflict is the Pauline 'flesh' (Galatians 5:19-21: the desires of the flesh include enmity, strife, jealousy, outbursts of anger, dissensions, divisions). Christ's diagnosis of the human problem: 'out of the heart come evil thoughts, murder, adultery, sexual immorality, theft, false witness, slander' (Matthew 15:19). The quarrels in the community are symptoms of the deep war within unrenewed human nature — a war that only the Spirit of Christ can resolve (Galatians 5:16: walk by the Spirit and you will not gratify the desires of the flesh).
2: 'You desire and do not have, so you murder. You covet and cannot obtain, so you fight and quarrel. You do not have because you do not ask God' — the escalation from desire to murder to asking is James's compressed moral anthropology. The failure to ask God is the failure to rely on Christ as the source of all good gifts (James 1:17; John 15:7: if you abide in me and my words abide in you, ask whatever you wish and it will be done for you). The community that fights over what it wants has forgotten that Christ is the one from whose abundance all good things come — making competitive grasping unnecessary.
3: 'You ask and do not receive, because you ask wrongly — wanting to spend it on your own pleasures' — the wrong asking is asking for the satisfaction of the hēdonai (pleasures) that are themselves the problem. Christ's model for right asking: 'Your kingdom come, your will be done on earth as it is in heaven' (Matthew 6:10) — asking that is aligned with God's purposes rather than personal gratification. The promise of James 4:2 (ask and you will receive) is conditioned by the alignment of the request with the will of Christ (1 John 5:14: if we ask anything according to his will, he hears us).
4: 'You adulterous people! Do you not know that friendship with the world is hostility toward God? Therefore, whoever chooses to be a friend of the world becomes an enemy of God' — the adultery metaphor frames the community's relationship with Christ as marital: the covenant relationship broken by worldly friendship is the covenant between Christ and the church (Ephesians 5:25-32; Revelation 19:7). Christ himself demanded undivided allegiance: 'No one can serve two masters' (Matthew 6:24); 'Whoever is not with me is against me' (Matthew 12:30). The double-mindedness of 1:8 here reaches its ultimate Christological definition: double-mindedness is choosing the world over Christ.
5: 'Or do you think Scripture speaks to no purpose? 'The Spirit God made to dwell in us longs jealously'' — the divine jealousy that the Spirit expresses is the jealousy of a God who has given himself in Christ and therefore claims exclusive loyalty. The God who 'did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all' (Romans 8:32) has expressed love that warrants undivided return. The indwelling Spirit of Christ (Romans 8:9) is the personal presence of the divine jealousy — Christ himself dwelling in the believer and longing for their undivided devotion.
6: 'But he gives all the more grace. That is why Scripture says: 'God opposes the proud but shows favor to the humble'' (Proverbs 3:34) — the 'all the more grace' (meizōna de didōsin charin) is the superabundant grace of Christ who 'poured out on us richly' (Titus 3:6) the Spirit. The humble person who receives this grace is the one who has adopted the Christ-pattern: 'he humbled himself and became obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross' (Philippians 2:8). God's favor to the humble is realized supremely in raising the crucified Christ — the ultimate vindication of humility.
7: 'Submit yourselves therefore to God. Resist the devil, and he will flee from you' — the two movements are the Christological program. Submission to God mirrors Christ's own submission: 'Not as I will, but as you will' (Matthew 26:39); 'I always do what pleases him' (John 8:29). Resistance of the devil mirrors Christ's own temptation-victory: 'Away with you, Satan!' (Matthew 4:10), after which 'the devil left him' (James 4:11). The believer who resists the devil in Christ's name and with Christ's word (Christ rebuked Satan with Scripture three times in the wilderness) finds the same result: the devil flees.
8: 'Draw near to God, and he will draw near to you. Cleanse your hands, you sinners, and purify your hearts, you double-minded' — the promise 'he will draw near to you' is the promise of the incarnation writ in miniature: God who drew near in Christ (John 1:14: the Word became flesh and dwelt among us) continues to draw near through the Spirit to those who turn to him. The cleansing of hands and purifying of hearts echoes Psalms 24:3-4 (who shall ascend the hill of the LORD? He who has clean hands and a pure heart). Christ fulfilled this Psalm as the one of perfectly clean hands and pure heart who ascended the hill of Calvary and then the hill of the Lord.
9: 'Be wretched and mourn and weep. Let your laughter be turned to mourning and your joy to grief' — the call to mourning is the call to true repentance, the grief that the beatitudes promise will issue in comfort: 'Blessed are those who mourn, for they will be comforted' (Matthew 5:4). The Christological movement: the mourning of genuine repentance precedes the joy of reconciliation with God — just as the disciples' grief at the cross (John 16:20: you will weep and lament, but the world will rejoice) was turned to joy at the resurrection (James 16:20: but your grief will turn to joy). James is not opposing joy but ordering it: grief before restoration, not laughter that avoids reckoning.
10: 'Humble yourselves before the Lord, and he will lift you up' — the promise of being 'lifted up' is the promise of exaltation by the God who exalts the humble. Christ himself is the supreme paradigm: 'he humbled himself and became obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross. Therefore God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name' (Philippians 2:8-9). The believer who follows the Christological pattern of self-humbling receives the Christological promise of divine exaltation — not through their own achievement but through participation in Christ's own humiliation-and-exaltation pattern.
11: 'Do not speak evil against one another, brothers. Whoever speaks evil against a brother or judges his brother speaks evil against the law and judges it' — the anti-slander command grounds brother-judgment in law-judgment, because the law that says 'love your neighbor' is being violated when a brother is slandered. The Christological extension: Christ said 'do not judge, and you will not be judged' (Matthew 7:1); he alone has the right to judge because he is the standard by which all judgment will be made (John 5:22: the Father has entrusted all judgment to the Son). To judge a brother is to usurp the prerogative of Christ.
12: 'There is only one Lawgiver and Judge — the one who is able to save and destroy. But who are you to judge your neighbor?' — the singular Lawgiver who is also Judge is Christ, to whom the Father has committed all judgment (John 5:22-23) and who is 'able to save completely those who come to God through him' (Hebrews 7:25). The one 'able to save and destroy' (sōsai kai apolysai) is language that describes absolute judicial sovereignty — the sovereignty that belongs to Christ the coming Judge (2 Timothy 4:1; Acts 10:42). The community that usurps this role by judging one another has displaced the Judge who alone has legitimate authority.
13: 'Come now, you who say, 'Today or tomorrow we will go to this city and spend a year there doing business and making a profit'' — the self-confident commercial planning that James addresses presupposes a mastery of time and circumstance that belongs only to Christ. 'All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me' (Matthew 28:18) — the one who possesses all authority over the future is Christ; the merchant who plans without reference to Christ's will has implicitly claimed that authority for himself. The 'come now' (ἄγε νῦν) of 4:13 echoes James 5:1, framing both the arrogant rich and the arrogant planner within the same prophetic rebuke.
14: 'Yet you do not know what tomorrow will bring. What is your life? For you are a mist that appears for a little while and then vanishes' — the transience of human life that James evokes (Psalms 39:5-6; James 144:4: man is like a breath, his days are like a passing shadow) is the Christological invitation to invest life in what is eternal. Christ made the same argument through the parable of the rich fool: 'This very night your life will be demanded from you. Then who will get what you have prepared for yourself?' (Luke 12:20). The mist that vanishes is contrasted with Christ himself, 'the same yesterday and today and forever' (Hebrews 13:8).
15: 'Instead you ought to say, 'If the Lord wills (ean ho kyrios thelēsē), we will live and do this or that'' — the posture of 'if the Lord wills' is the Gethsemane posture: 'Not as I will, but as you will' (Matthew 26:39; James 26:42: your will be done). Christ himself lived with complete submission to the Father's will (John 5:30: I seek not my own will but the will of him who sent me; James 6:38: I have come down from heaven not to do my will but the will of him who sent me). The believer who adopts the 'if the Lord wills' posture is adopting the Christological disposition of voluntary submission to divine sovereignty over time and circumstance.
16: 'But as it is, you boast in your arrogance. All such boasting is evil' — the boasting (kauchaō) in arrogant self-confidence is the opposite of Paul's 'boasting in the cross of our Lord Jesus Christ' (Galatians 6:14). The evil of arrogant boasting is its displacement of Christ from the center: the boaster has made himself the author and sovereign of his own life, usurping the role of the Lord who is 'the Lord our God, the sovereign over all kings of the earth' (Revelation 17:14). The antidote is not self-deprecation but the shift of boasting from self to Christ — the one in whom the believer's true identity and security rest.
17: 'So whoever knows the right thing to do and fails to do it, for that person it is sin' — the sin of omission as the counterpart to the sin of commission: James ends his practical ethics with the most demanding test. Knowing the right thing and not doing it is the failure of the living faith that James has argued throughout. The one who 'knows' what Christ requires (love your neighbor, care for the widow and orphan, speak truthfully, plan with humility) and does not do it has failed the fundamental test of genuine discipleship. Christ's own word: 'If you know these things, blessed are you if you do them' (John 13:17) — the blessing belongs to those for whom knowing and doing are not separated.
